chapters.title: Foundations
chapters.framework: C is a typed language. Every piece of data must have a type that tells the compiler how much memory to allocate: 'int' gets 4 bytes, 'double' gets 8 bytes.

chapters.title: Control Flow
chapters.framework: Execution flow is governed by logic gates like if/else and loops. These determine which instructions the CPU processes next based on register comparisons.

chapters.title: Memory & Pointers
chapters.framework: A pointer is simply a variable that stores a memory address. Think of it as a remote control that cannot do anything itself but tells you where the real TV is located.

chapters.title: Function Logic
chapters.framework: Functions create local stack frames. Recursive calls stack these frames atop each other until a base case is hit, then they unwind in LIFO order.

chapters.title: Dynamic Allocation
chapters.framework: Heap memory is persistent until freed. Unlike the stack, which cleans up after a function finishes, heap memory stays reserved as long as you need it.
